topic: Arrays - map, each_ref, as_slice, and flatten

turns.content: I'm trying to process an array of numbers in my Rust side project but I keep getting allocation errors when I try to transform the data. I thought using a standard loop with push_back would be faster since it avoids creating new heap memory.

That's actually where many beginners get tripped up; you don't need to allocate at all for fixed-size arrays because the map function operates directly on stack data without touching the heap. If you're working with a concrete array like [i32; 5], calling array.map(f) will produce a new [ResultType; N] immediately, keeping everything zero-cost.
Wait so I can just call map on an existing array and get another array back without any overhead? That sounds too good to be true.

It is exactly that efficient because arrays are stack-based collections with fixed size N, so the map method returns a new array of the same length without needing malloc or free calls. However, if you need references to those elements instead of moving them, you should use each_ref which creates an array of references [&T; N] rather than copying the values.

I see now about the difference between owning the data versus just referencing it, but I'm stuck on how to pass this flat 2D array structure as a slice when my function expects &[T]. It seems like there's no way to coerce a nested array into a simple slice without copying everything.

You don't need to copy anything because as_slice() will automatically coerce that nested array into the required slice type for you. It handles the coercion from [[T; M]; N] directly to &[T] without any manual iteration or reallocation.
That's surprisingly convenient, but what if I have a deeply nested structure and need to flatten it into one big array? Can I just chain aslice calls or do I really need the nightly flatten trait?

For simple cases you can manually iterate with iter() to collect elements, but if your compiler supports it via nightly features then flatten() on a 2D array like [[T; M]; N] will produce a single [T; N*M] in one step. That avoids the accumulation overhead of manual flattening loops.

Okay so I understand that arrays avoid heap allocations entirely and there are specific methods for different use cases, but I still feel frustrated because my first attempt at this was so wrong. How do I build the intuition to spot these patterns quickly instead of just guessing?

The best way is to recognize that when you need a new array with transformed values, use map; when you need immutable access without copying, use each_ref; and when you need slice semantics or flattening, rely on as_slice or flatten. Once you internalize which tool solves which problem, the frustration turns into confidence.
